startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 55-year-old man with a history of chronic alcohol use presents with jaundice and hepatomegaly. Work through this case applying your knowledge of oncogenes, tumour suppressors, HIV virology, and alcohol metabolism.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified the biochemical basis of alcoholic liver disease and demonstrated strong knowledge of molecular oncology and virology.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review the roles of oncogenes, tumour suppressors, reverse transcriptase, and alcohol metabolism.
Revisit the fundamentals of proto-oncogene activation, p53 function, HIV replication, and ethanol oxidation pathways.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 55-year-old man presents with jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, and ascites. He admits to drinking heavily for 20 years. His liver biopsy shows macrovesicular steatosis and Mallory-Denk bodies.

Meanwhile, his blood work reveals elevated liver enzymes. You recall that chronic alcohol consumption causes liver damage because ethanol is oxidised to acetaldehyde by alcohol dehydrogenase, increasing the NADH:NAD+ ratio and promoting fatty acid synthesis.

The patient also asks about a family member recently diagnosed with colon cancer involving a RAS mutation — an oncogene mutated in approximately 30% of all human cancers.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient's family member has a colon tumour with a RAS mutation. When a proto-oncogene mutates to become an oncogene, what is the fundamental change in cell signalling?

alternatives.text: The growth signal becomes permanently active, driving uncontrolled proliferation
The cell loses the ability to undergo apoptosis via p53

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Loss of apoptosis relates to tumour suppressor gene (p53) dysfunction, not oncogene activation. RAS is a proto-oncogene that becomes a permanently active growth signal when mutated.

alternatives.text: The cell begins producing reverse transcriptase

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Reverse transcriptase is an enzyme used by retroviruses like HIV, not a product of oncogene activation. Oncogenes produce permanently active growth signals.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! Oncogenes produce permanently active growth signals. You now investigate the patient further.

Biopsy of a suspicious hepatic nodule reveals hepatocellular carcinoma. Immunohistochemistry shows absent p53 staining — the guardian of the genome. When DNA damage is irreparable, p53 normally triggers apoptosis (programmed cell death) to eliminate damaged cells.

Additionally, the patient is found to be HIV-positive. HIV is a retrovirus that uses reverse transcriptase to convert its RNA genome into DNA, which integrates into the host genome using integrase.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient is HIV-positive and has hepatocellular carcinoma with absent p53. Which combination correctly describes the key molecular events?

alternatives.text: HIV uses reverse transcriptase and integrase; p53 loss removes the apoptosis checkpoint for damaged cells
HIV uses DNA polymerase; p53 loss causes increased NADH production

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: HIV uses reverse transcriptase (not DNA polymerase) to convert RNA to DNA. NADH:NAD+ ratio changes are due to alcohol metabolism, not p53 dysfunction.

alternatives.text: HIV integrates via helicase; p53 loss activates RAS oncogene

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: HIV integration uses integrase, not helicase. p53 loss and RAS activation are independent events — p53 is a tumour suppressor, while RAS is an oncogene.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! You have correctly identified the molecular mechanisms at play:

  • Alcoholic liver disease: Ethanol → acetaldehyde (via alcohol dehydrogenase), increased NADH:NAD+ ratio → fatty liver
  • Oncogenes: Mutated RAS = permanently active growth signal (~30% of cancers)
  • Tumour suppressors: p53 = guardian of the genome; triggers apoptosis when DNA damage is irreparable
  • HIV virology: Retrovirus using reverse transcriptase (RNA→DNA) and integrase (genome integration)

This case illustrates how alcohol-induced liver damage, loss of tumour suppressor function, and viral co-infection can converge in hepatocellular carcinoma.
